sections.text: Welcome back to AP Psychology. Module Nine — motivation and emotion. This is the unit that explains why you cannot make yourself open the textbook, but you can spend four hours making the perfect Spotify playlist. By the end of today you'll know the major theories of why we DO things, and the four big theories of how feelings actually work. Let's get into it.

Quick experiment on yourself. You sit down to study for this exam. You last about eleven minutes. Then you're on your phone. But last weekend, you spent four solid hours building the perfect Spotify playlist for a road trip nobody asked you to plan. Same brain. Same person. Wildly different effort. Why? The textbook is extrinsic — somebody else assigned it, and the reward is a grade in the future. The playlist is intrinsic — you did it because YOU wanted to, and the doing was the reward. That gap between extrinsic and intrinsic motivation is one of the most studied things in psychology. Today we figure out how it works.

Three things today. One — the major theories of motivation, from old-school instincts to Maslow's pyramid to self-determination theory. Two — the four theories of emotion, which is the AP exam's favorite trap because students mix them up constantly. Three — universal emotions across cultures, plus why polygraph tests don't actually detect lies. Let's go.

Start with two old-school theories. Drive-reduction theory — your body wants homeostasis, balance. When you're hungry, hunger is a drive that pushes you to eat until balance is restored. When you're thirsty, you drink. Behavior reduces the drive. Simple, biological, and great for explaining hunger and thirst — but useless for explaining why anyone would skydive or watch a horror movie. That's where arousal theory comes in. Sometimes we don't want to REDUCE arousal, we want to INCREASE it. Bored kids climb things. The Yerkes-Dodson law says there's an optimal level of arousal for performance — and it depends on the task. For an easy task — sprinting, basic recall — high arousal helps. For a hard task — calculus, this AP exam — moderate arousal is best. Too little, you're sleepy. Too much, you're panicking. The sweet spot is in the middle. AP loves Yerkes-Dodson. Memorize the inverted-U.

Now the most famous picture in introductory psychology. Maslow's hierarchy of needs. Five levels stacked as a pyramid. Bottom level — physiological — food, water, sleep, oxygen. The basics. Without these, nothing else matters. Level two — safety — shelter, security, predictability. Level three — belonging and love — friends, family, romantic connection. Level four — esteem — feeling competent, respected, accomplished. Top level — self-actualization — becoming the fullest version of yourself, whatever that means for you. Maslow's claim — you mostly work on the lower needs first. A starving person isn't optimizing their career. The picture is intuitive and AP loves the order. The honest caveat — newer research says people don't actually climb the pyramid neatly. Hungry people still need love. But for the exam, know the five levels in order from bottom to top.

The modern upgrade is self-determination theory, from Deci and Ryan in the nineteen eighties. Their claim — humans have three core psychological needs. Autonomy — feeling like you chose this. Competence — feeling like you're good at it, or getting better. Relatedness — feeling connected to other people. When all three are met, you get intrinsic motivation — you do the thing because the doing itself feels good. When they're not met, you need extrinsic motivation — rewards, grades, paychecks, threats — to get the same behavior. Big finding — extrinsic rewards can actually KILL intrinsic motivation. Pay a kid to read books they already love, and they'll often stop reading when the money stops. That's called the overjustification effect. The brain says, oh, I guess I was only doing this for the money. So when you build a school, or a workplace, or a study habit — the goal is to support autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not just throw rewards at people.

Now switch gears. Emotion. Four theories — and they all answer the same question in different orders. The question is — what comes first, the body or the feeling? Theory one — James-Lange, eighteen eighties. Body first. You see a snake, your heart races, your legs run, and THEN your brain interprets all that and says, oh, I must be afraid. The famous line — I run, therefore I'm afraid. Theory two — Cannon-Bard. They said no, the body and the feeling happen at the same time, in parallel. The thalamus splits the signal — one copy goes to the body to make your heart pound, another copy goes to the cortex to feel fear. Simultaneous. Theory three — Schachter-Singer two-factor. They said you need both — physical arousal AND a cognitive label. Your heart pounds. You look around. If there's a bear, you label the arousal as fear. If you just chugged espresso, you might label the same arousal as excitement. Same body, different stories, different emotions. Theory four — Lazarus cognitive appraisal. Cognition comes FIRST. Before you even feel arousal, your brain has already appraised the situation as threat or not-threat. The appraisal triggers the emotion. These four are the AP trap of the unit. Side-by-side comparison coming up.

Your turn. You walk into a dark room. Your heart pounds. You notice your heart pounding. You glance at the corner, see a figure, and label what you're feeling as fear. Two seconds later your roommate flips on the light, you realize it's a coat rack, and the fear melts away. Question — which theory of emotion best fits THIS sequence? Pause the video. Decide. Press play.

Welcome back. The answer is Schachter-Singer, the two-factor theory. Why? Because you noticed the arousal first — pounding heart — and then you LABELED it based on the situation. Coat rack? Fear melts. Bear? Fear stays. The arousal alone wasn't enough — your brain needed a cognitive label to turn it into a specific emotion. Quick comparison. James-Lange would say the heart pounding IS the fear, no labeling needed. Cannon-Bard would say the pounding heart and the feeling of fear happened at the exact same instant. Lazarus would say you appraised the dark room as threatening BEFORE the heart pounded. Schachter-Singer is the only one that requires arousal plus a cognitive label, in that order. The phrase to lock in — two factors — physical arousal plus cognitive interpretation — equals emotion.

Are emotions universal? Mostly, yes. Paul Ekman's classic research, nineteen sixties and seventies — he showed photos of facial expressions to people in completely different cultures, including remote tribes in New Guinea who had no exposure to Western media. He found six emotions that were recognized everywhere. Happy. Sad. Fear. Anger. Disgust. Surprise. Same face, same name, every culture. The takeaway — these six emotions are biologically wired. They're not learned. Babies show them, blind kids who never SAW a smile still smile. But — display rules differ. WHEN it's appropriate to show an emotion, and how big to show it, varies a lot by culture. In some cultures you cry openly at funerals. In others you hold it in. The emotion is universal. The performance isn't.

One more big idea. Polygraph tests. The lie detector. They're famous, they're on TV, and they don't actually detect lies. What a polygraph measures is arousal — heart rate, breathing, skin conductance. The theory is that liars get more aroused than truth-tellers. Problem one — guilty people who are calm under pressure pass. Problem two — innocent people who are nervous about being tested fail. Problem three — different emotions all produce similar arousal. Fear, guilt, anger, embarrassment, even excitement, all light up the polygraph the same way. Most courts in the U.S. don't allow polygraph results as evidence for that exact reason. The validity is too low. AP loves this — when they ask about the polygraph, the answer is always — it measures arousal, not lying.

Where you've already seen this. Every video game with a daily reward streak — that's variable-ratio reinforcement plus an attempt to give you autonomy and competence. Every diet that fails after the cash bonus runs out — overjustification effect. Every time a coach says relax before a free throw — Yerkes-Dodson, dialing arousal down for a precise task. Every time you laugh at a funny situation only AFTER you realize you're safe — that's cognitive appraisal sliding into your emotional response. This unit is everywhere once you know it.

Recap. Six things. One — drive-reduction explains hunger and thirst. Arousal theory explains why we sometimes seek excitement, and Yerkes-Dodson says the optimal arousal level depends on task difficulty. Two — Maslow's hierarchy stacks five needs from physiological at the base to self-actualization at the top. Three — self-determination theory says we need autonomy, competence, and relatedness to be intrinsically motivated. Extrinsic rewards can backfire — that's the overjustification effect. Four — four theories of emotion. James-Lange — body first, feeling follows. Cannon-Bard — body and feeling happen at the same time. Schachter-Singer — arousal plus cognitive label. Lazarus — cognitive appraisal comes first. Five — Ekman's six universal emotions are recognized across cultures. Six — polygraphs measure arousal, not lying. Low validity. Don't trust the TV version.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master Module Nine, here's the path. One — read Myers Psychology for AP, Module Nine on motivation and emotion. Pay especially close attention to the four emotion theories — that side-by-side is the most-tested chart in this unit. Two — go to AP Classroom and do twelve multiple-choice questions on identifying emotion theories from short scenarios. Most students lose points here by mixing up Cannon-Bard and Schachter-Singer. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: read eight scenarios and label each one as James-Lange, Cannon-Bard, Schachter-Singer, or Lazarus. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if Maslow versus self-determination still feels fuzzy. They overlap, but they're not the same. See you in Module Ten — developmental psychology, where we follow a human from a clump of cells to old age and figure out what changes when.
